text: While such a unit might seem expensive at first (the cost would be about $150,000 to $200,000), it would give an important tool for study at a cost not too much more than a cyclotron and with a much better source.

text: Morrison is a slow chain reaction in 49, using a homogeneous mixture of 49 and a moderator with a central core.

text: Therefore, it is suggested that beryllium or beryllium oxide or some similar material be used as a moderator with the 49 probably in the form of the oxide.

section: Do Not Transfer To Another Person
text: With beryllium oxide as the moderator and $2 - 3\mathrm{kg}$ of 49 in the form of the oxide as the active metal, the temperature drops across the pile would be about $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

text: Surrounding this to form the pile proper would be a cube 80 cm on a side composed of closely fitted beryllium oxide blocks with small depressions at regular intervals into which would be placed small compressed bricks of the plutonium oxide (pure beryllium blocks and pure 49 could be used in place of the oxide).

section: Do Not Transfer To Another Person
text: Surrounding the cubical pile would be a 2" thick graphite crucible contained within a shell of bismuth-lead eutectic covering all sides of the pile and crucible except the top.

section: Do Not Transfer To Another Person
text: Wigner feared that the beryllium might reduce the oxide leaving pure 49 which melts at $625^{\circ}$ and therefore it would be preferable to either use oxides for both the moderator and the active metal or else the pure metals.

text: Morrison explained that the specific activity in the P-9 pile at Argonne was rather low and that the pile he had designed had the advantage of not depending upon neutron capture to obtain high specific activity.
